5: enikesen ho leon ho ek tes phyles Iouda e riza David anoixai to biblion: 'The Lion of the tribe of Judah, the Root of David, has conquered, so that he can open the scroll.' The title-cluster is Christological recapitulation: Leon ek phyles Iouda (Lion-of-Judah: Genesis 49:9-10), Riza David (Root of David: Isaiah 11:1, 10; Revelation 22:16), enikesen (has conquered). Then the stunning inversion: the 'Lion' who conquers is revealed as a 'Lamb standing as if slain' (Revelation 5:6). John hears Lion, then sees Lamb — the conquering is through the cross, not through military force. This Lion-Lamb inversion is Revelation's central hermeneutic: power is redefined by the slain Lamb who stands.
1: kai eidon epi tēn dexian tou kathēmenou epi tou thronou biblion gegrammenon esōthen kai opisthen, katesphraismenon sphragisin hepta: 'I saw in the right hand of him seated on the throne a scroll written inside and on the back, sealed with seven seals.' Esōthen kai opisthen (inside and on the back): an opisthographon — a scroll written on both sides. Such scrolls were rare and indicated a document so full in content it overflowed the standard single-side. Seven seals: divine completeness/perfection of the sealing — the scroll's contents are fully secured and cannot be accessed until the seals are broken by the worthy one.
2: kai eidon aggelon ischyron kēryssonta en phōnē megalē; Tis axios anoixai to biblion kai lysai tas sphragas autou: 'I saw a mighty angel proclaiming with a loud voice: Who is worthy to open the scroll and break its seals?' Ischuron aggelon (mighty angel): this figure appears again in 10:1 and Revelation 18:21 — a supernaturally powerful heavenly being whose proclamation traverses the entire cosmos (Revelation 5:3). Axios (worthy): the scroll-crisis is a worthiness test; no one passes initially, intensifying the revelation of the Lamb's singular qualification.
3: kai oudeis edynato en tō ouranō oude epi tēs gēs oude hypokatō tēs gēs anoixai to biblion oude blepein auto: 'No one in heaven or on earth or under the earth was able to open the scroll or to look into it.' The tripartite cosmos (heaven, earth, underworld) is searched exhaustively — no created being anywhere in existence meets the criterion. The totality of the failure intensifies the worthiness of the one who does step forward.
4: kai eklaion poly, hoti oudeis axios heurethē anoixai to biblion oude blepein auto: 'I wept greatly because no one was found worthy to open the scroll or to look into it.' Eklaion (imperfect): ongoing weeping, not a single cry. The imperfect captures John's sustained distress while the search proved fruitless. The scroll contains the plan of God for history (Revelation 6-22 unfold its contents) — if no one can open it, history has no redemptive resolution. John weeps for the apparent inaccessibility of God's purpose.
6: arnion hestēkos hōs esphagmenon, echon kerata hepta kai ophthalmous hepta: 'a Lamb standing, as though it had been slain, with seven horns and seven eyes.' Arnion (little lamb): Revelation's preferred word for Christ (29 times), distinct from amnos (used in John 1:29; 1 Peter 1:19). The diminutive arnion emphasizes smallness and vulnerability. Hōs esphagmenon (as if slain): the wounds of the cross are visible in the glorified body — the Lamb bears permanent marks of sacrifice. Hestēkos (standing, perfect participle): it was slain but stands — resurrection expressed in a participle. Seven horns (omnipotence, via the OT horn-as-power symbol) + seven eyes (omniscience, identified as the seven Spirits of God) = the slain Lamb who is simultaneously omnipotent and omniscient.
7: kai ēlthen kai eilēphen ek tēs dexias tou kathēmenou epi tou thronou: 'He came and took it from the right hand of him seated on the throne.' Two aorists in sequence: ēlthen (came) and eilēphen (took). The taking is a decisive act — the Lamb approaches and receives the scroll from God's hand. In the context of the worthiness contest (Revelation 5:2-4), this single action resolves the cosmic crisis: the one worthy to open history's scroll is the slain Lamb, and he takes what belongs to him.
8: phialas chrysas gemousas thymiamaton, hai eisin hai proseuchai tōn hagiōn: 'golden bowls full of incense, which are the prayers of the saints.' The identification is explicit: the incense in the heavenly temple is the accumulated prayers of the community on earth. In the OT, incense was offered on the golden altar before the Most Holy Place (Exodus 30:1-10) as a symbol of prayers ascending to God. Here the twenty-four elders and four living creatures hold the very prayers of the church — every prayer uttered on earth has a heavenly counterpart in these bowls. Kitharan (harp): liturgical instrument, the instrument of Levitical temple worship (1 Chronicles 25:1-8).
9: ōdēn kainēn: 'a new song.' The ōdē kainē formula (Psalms 96:1; Revelation 98:1; Revelation 144:9; Isaiah 42:10) marks the song as celebrating a new redemptive act of God — not repetition of a known melody but improvised praise for an unprecedented deed. Hoti esphagēs kai ēgorasas tō haimati sou (because you were slain and purchased with your blood): the ground of the new song is the cross. Ēgorasas (you purchased): the aorist of agorazō (to buy in the marketplace) — redemption as purchase, the language of manumission. The price was Christ's blood.
10: kai epoiēsas autous tō theō hēmōn basileian kai hiereis kai basileusousin epi tēs gēs: 'You made them a kingdom and priests to our God, and they shall reign on the earth.' The repetition of Exodus 19:6 (cf. Revelation 1:6) now placed in the mouth of the heavenly choir as a completed fact: the cross has constituted the redeemed community as kingdom-priests. Basileusousin epi tēs gēs (they shall reign on the earth): future tense — the eschatological reign has not yet arrived but is secured; the kingdom is real and its earthly expression is coming.
11: kai ēkousa phōnēn aggelōn pollōn kyklothen tou thronou kai tōn zōōn kai tōn presbyterōn, kai ēn ho arithmos autōn myriades myriadōn kai chiliades chiliadōn: 'I heard around the throne the voice of many angels, and their number was myriads of myriads and thousands of thousands.' Myriades myriadōn kai chiliades chiliadōn: drawn from Daniel 7:10's description of the heavenly court (ten thousand times ten thousand stood before him). The number is maximally indefinite — uncountable multitudes. The witness expands from the four living creatures and twenty-four elders (Revelation 5:8) to the full angelic choir.
12: axios estin to arnion to esphagmenon labein tēn dynamin kai plouton kai sophian kai ischyn kai timēn kai doxan kai eulogian: 'Worthy is the Lamb who was slain, to receive power and wealth and wisdom and might and honor and glory and blessing.' Seven attributes in the doxology (seven = completeness): dynamis, ploutos, sophia, ischus, timē, doxa, eulogia. This sevenfold doxology to the Lamb matches the threefold doxology to God in Revelation 5:11 and the fourfold in Revelation 5:13 — the deliberate numerical escalation reaching seven for the Lamb signals the fullness of divine honor directed to Christ. Ho esphagmenon (the slain one): the perfect participle — the slaying is a permanent defining characteristic of the Lamb's identity even in glory.
13: pasan ktistin hē en tō ouranō kai epi tēs gēs kai hypokatō tēs gēs kai epi tēs thalassēs kai ta en autois: 'Every creature in heaven and on earth and under the earth and in the sea and all that is in them.' The fourfold enumeration surpasses even the triple-cosmos of Revelation 5:3 — the sea is added as a fourth domain. The scope is absolute: no creature is excluded from the worship that concludes Revelation 5. The doxology to 'him who sits on the throne and to the Lamb' places the Lamb as co-recipient of cosmic worship with God.
14: kai ta tessara zōia elegon Amēn: 'And the four living creatures said Amen.' Amēn as the liturgical seal of the cosmic doxology: the four living creatures who represent all creation affirm what every creature has sung. Kai hoi presbyteroi epesan kai prosekynesan: and the elders fell down and worshiped — the same prostration as in Revelation 5:8, now as the conclusion of the entire throne-room scene. The chapter ends in worship; Revelation's entire eschatological vision is framed by and resolved into worship of the Lamb.
